full_text: PSG spent seven years building their entire club around Kylian Mbappe and never won the Champions League. That very season he left, Enrique's team lifted the Champions League trophy. Only two days before he signed up Madrid, Real Madrid lifted the Champions League trophy, not because of one superstar, but because of a team that came back from the dead, against Bayern Munich, against Manchester City, through pure collective will and a never-say-die mentality that no individual stat can measure. Then Mbappe signed, the numbers went up, the trophies stopped coming, PSG, Madrid, same player, same result. In this video, we're breaking down exactly why Real Madrid got worse after signing Mbappe, and it goes a lot deeper than tactics. To understand what went wrong, you have to understand what was already there. In 2023-24, Real Madrid won the Champions League, and La Liga, without a recognised world-class striker, no superstar number nine, no marquee signing, just a system, built on collective pressing, fast transitions, and a midfield that could control any game on the planet. Jude Bellingham arriving late into the box, Venetius Jr tearing apart left flanks with nobody to crowd him out, and at the centre of everything, Tony Cross, sitting deep, 92% pass accuracy, dictating the tempo of games, like a conductor who never rushed a note. He didn't just hold the structure together, he was the structure. In the way that he plays the game, there is a simplicity to Tony Cross' game that actually separates him from the rest, because he simply finds the best pass available all the time. And you think of him as a guy who can pass the ball, sideways and back, of course, but the switch in the point of attack, the penetrating passes, and I think his biggest attribute and his biggest asset as a player is the understanding of when to slow him down and when to speed it up. Then, Cross retired. And this is where Florentino Perez faced the defining decision of this era, the squad needed a brain, a holding midfielder who could replace the control Cross gave them, someone to manage the space between the lines, while Bellingham pushed forward, that's what the football demanded. Instead, the board spent on Mbappe. Now that alone isn't necessarily wrong. The problem is Hu Mbappe is tactically, he doesn't function as a traditional number nine, he refuses to pin defenders, rarely holds a play, and gravitates naturally toward holding the left half space. The exact territory of Inicius Jr. had spent three years turning into the most dangerous corridor in world football. Nobody on the board asked the obvious question, what happens when you put two left side attackers who both refused to be selfless in the same system? The answer was coming, and it wasn't pretty. I said it last year, at the end of the season, when we knew that Mbappe was going there, how is it going to balance that team? The left wing collision was immediate, and it never got resolved. Inicius averaged 189 progressive carries in 2025-26, and Mbappe recorded 90, and yet both of them were chasing the same five yard strip of grass on the left side of the pitch, game after game. Out of over 1300 passes, Inicius made across the season, only 15% went to Mbappe, the number going in the other direction wasn't much better. That isn't a partnership, that's two players running parallel careers on the same team. They're never quite linking, never quite trusting, occupying the same space and cancelling each other out. And the player who paid the biggest price for it, it wasn't either of them, it was Bellingham. Thierry Henry said it plainly on air during one Champions League broadcast, Bellingham was making the runs into the box that a number nine is supposed to make, while the actual number nine stood somewhere between the left channel and the edge of the opposition half, not pressing, not pulling defenders, just waiting. So Bellingham dropped deeper to compensate, covered wider to fill gaps, and his goal output collapsed as a result. A player who scored 23 league goals in his debut season became a utility piece, patching structural holes that Mbappe's presence created. The defensive numbers tell the rest of the story. All recoveries dropped from 46.5 per game to 40.6. In certain Champions League matches against Arsenal, Madrid were outrun by up to 14km, the rough equivalent of playing the entire game, a man's short. Gaghan pressing efficiency fell from 29.9% to 25%, which meant that every time Madrid lost the ball in the middle third, opponents had a clean two second window to exploit the space behind a midfield, with no protective cover. Mbappe recorded nine tackles across the entire 2025 campaign, Rodrigo, a player with half his platform and a fraction of his contract, recorded 19 in the same period. When the man at the top of the press doesn't press, the line behind him doesn't hold, and when that line doesn't hold with no cross sitting behind it, the team is defending from a position of constant emergency. Here's the part that stings most. In stretches where Mbappe was absent, Madrid's win percentage in La Liga actually climbed to 80%. The shape came back, Venetius found his territory again. The team remembered had a function as a unit, then Mbappe returned, and the problems returned with him, same as they always had at every club before this one. Are these statistics not quite impacted? I think that is the question that Mbappe left to ask himself. Is he impacting the team, or is he just scoring goals? But I appreciate how stupid that is to say, in a sport where the object is to go and score as many goals as possible, that maybe the goals are not quite delivering the impact that you would expect from a player like Mbappe. The tactical issues were real, measurable, and consistent, but they were symptoms, not the disease. The real rot was happening somewhere else entirely. January, 2026, Chabi Alonso, a coach who understood pressing systems, who built his entire identity around defensive discipline, and collective structure, was fired mid-season. The results weren't catastrophic, the football wasn't broken beyond repair, but the locker room had fractured, and the fracture ran directly through one relationship, Alonso and Mbappe. Reports coming out of Valdababus painted a picture that anyone who followed PSG between 2019 and 2024 would recognize immediately. Senior players felt Alonso was too rigid with the superstar, too demanding, too unwilling to bend the system around the needs of a generational talent. When the board sided with the players over the coach, the decision sent a message to everyone inside that building. The manager is not the authority here, the star isn't touchable, and once that message lands, no tactical setup in the world fixes it. This isn't the first time this carousel has turned. At PSG, Thomas Tuchel built a genuinely structured side, took them to a Champions League final and was forced by the club to apologize directly to Mbappe after attempting to hold him accountable. The Killing in Mbappe and Rabeo both started on the bench, it was revealed afterwards, this was because of disciplinary reason, both were late to a team meeting as they wanted to watch the first half of Barcelona against Real Madrid. He left, and inside 18 months, won the Champions League with Chelsea. Pochettino replaced him and stopped coaching almost immediately, managing personalities instead of systems, watching the football drift further from any coherent identity. Galtier lasted barely a year, because the role he offered Mbappe didn't match promises made elsewhere, at Madrid the pattern didn't just repeat, it accelerated. Carlo Ancelotti's famous dressing room harmony, the glue, that held those Champions League nights together. Never fully recovered once Mbappe arrived, multiple analysts and pundits covering Madrid during this period described the squad as unmanageable, not because the talent disappeared, but because the power structure shifted, the manager stopped being the authority, the star became untouchable, and once that dynamic takes hold, no tactical setup in the world fixes it. Off the pitch, the leadership vacuum was just as damaging, when Cross and Modric were still around, the hierarchy ran itself, you knew who spoke first before a game, who took the penalty in the 88th minute, who pulled the younger players aside when standards dropped. That clarity disappeared, with Mbappe and Venetius both competing for top billing, the ship had no one at the wheel, friction over set pieces, over Hu led the huddle, over Hu's name the system was built around, those aren't small grievances. At Real Madrid, where the mental demands of a Champions League knockout stage are unlike anything else in club football, that kind of internal static costs you matches. Think about the way that Venetius Jr. changed this last year, which I think partially can be attributed to the fact that he didn't win the Ballon d'Or and wasn't pleased with it, can be attributed to the change within Mbappe. The Guard of Honor moment after the Spanish Super Cup lost to Barcelona, crystallized everything. Alonso asked the players to step forward, everyone moved except one. Mbappe refused, then, gestured to his teammates to walk away with him. Several did. It wasn't a spontaneous act of emotion, it was a demonstration of where the real authority sat. Alonso had already lost that dressing room, that moment just confirmed it for everyone watching. Florentino Perez spent heavily on a forward who was tactically redundant in the system he already had, and spent nothing on the holding midfielder the system desperately needed. And when you have players that, I mean, defy logic in how you get the dream team, and we see all these players here, Claude McAlele, Zizou Zendine Zidane said this about him when he left and went to Chelsea. He said, why put another layer of paint on a Bentley when you're losing the entire engine of this whole project? McAlele went on to win back-to-back Premier League titles, and was Chelsea's game, and was Madrid's loss? Real Madrid, how many trophies they won after he left in those three years? No, zero, nothing. PSG's evidence is the sharpest available mirror for what that decision costs. In Mbappe's final season, in Paris, the numbers were extraordinary, 44 goals, 10 assists. No Champions League. The year he left, Luis Enrique's PSG destroyed Inter, 5-0 in the final. Dembele won the Ballon d'Or. The team got better without their best player, because the structure was finally allowed to breathe, and Enrique had warned Mbappe directly. Michael Jordan defended like a madman. You don't get to lead without suffering, and Mbappe didn't change. Enrique didn't bend, and Enrique won everything. This isn't about one bad signing, it's about a club that temporarily forgot that its badge is bigger than the brand on the back of the shirt. Mbappe is one of the most lethal finishers the game has produced, that's not up for debate. But elite finishing and elite teamwork aren't the same thing, and Real Madrid learned that the hard way. PSG won their first Champions League the year he left, Madrid matched their worst defensive record the year he arrived. The data doesn't lie, it just takes time, before enough people are willing to read it honestly.
